perturbation.original_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall pay ABG a cash payment of: One Million Two Hundred Fifty Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,250,000) for Contract Year 1, One Million Three Hundred Seventy Five Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,375,000) for Contract Year 2, and One Million Five Hundred Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,500,000) for Contract Year 3 (such cash payments for Contract Years 1-3 referred to herein as the "Cash Payment") for the services and rights provided hereunder. In addition to the Cash Payment and any other compensation payable to ABG (and, as applicable, CELEBRITY) hereunder, PAPA JOHN'S shall pay without limitation, usage of the union-covered materials produced hereunder, with usage applied against the Cash Payment at double scale (or such other applicable rate). Each of such Cash Payments shall be paid in four equal installments, as set forth in Section 5.A.3 below, as follows: (A) The first payment is due on the Effective Date for the First Contract Year, and March 1 for subsequent Contract Years; (B) The second payment is due June 1 for each Contract Year; (C) The third payment is due September 1 for each Contract Year; and (D) The fourth payment is due December 1 for each Contract Year.
perturbation.changed_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall pay ABG a cash payment of: One Million Two Hundred Fifty Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,250,000) for Contract Year 1, One Million Three Hundred Seventy Five Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,375,000) for Contract Year 2, and One Million Five Hundred Thousand U.S. Dollars (USD$1,500,000) for Contract Year 3 (such cash payments for Contract Years 1-3 referred to herein as the "Cash Payment") for the services and rights provided hereunder. In addition to the Cash Payment and any other compensation payable to ABG (and, as applicable, CELEBRITY) hereunder, PAPA JOHN'S shall pay without limitation, usage of the union-covered materials produced hereunder, with usage applied against the Cash Payment at double scale (or such other applicable rate). Each of such Cash Payments shall be paid in four equal installments, as set forth in Section 5.A.3 below, as follows: (A) The first payment is due 90 days after the Effective Date for the First Contract Year, and 90 days after March 1 for subsequent Contract Years; (B) The second payment is due 90 days after June 1 for each Contract Year; (C) The third payment is due 90 days after September 1 for each Contract Year; and (D) The fourth payment is due 90 days after December 1 for each Contract Year.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the payment schedule to be 90 days after the specified dates can create issues with timely payment of wages/compensation, especially concerning union wages if any portion of the payments are allocated to union-covered services, and may violate wage payment laws, which generally require more frequent and timely payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: KRS § 337.055 mandates regular payment of wages, and while this contract might not be directly construed as wage payment, delaying the payment of compensation by 90 days after the initial agreed-upon dates could run afoul of the spirit and intent of ensuring timely and predictable compensation, especially since the contract mentions union fees and union-covered services. Delayed payments can trigger penalties and interest under Kentucky law if deemed a violation of prompt payment requirements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Kentucky Revised Statutes § 337.055 (2022) - Payment of all wages or salary upon dismissal or voluntary leaving required :: 2022 Kentucky Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Kentucky Rev. Stat. 2023 Kentucky Rev. Stat. 2022 Kentucky Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Kentucky Revised Statutes Chapter 337 - Wages and hours 337.055 Payment of all wages or salary upon dismissal or voluntary leaving required. Universal Citation: KY Rev Stat § 337.055 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Download as PDF 337.055 Payment of all wages or salary upon dismissal or voluntary leaving required. Any employee who leaves or is discharged from his employment shall be paid in full all wages or salary earned by him; not later than the next normal pay period following the date of dismissal or voluntary leaving or fourteen (14) days following such date of dismissal or voluntary leaving whichever last occurs. Any employee who is absent at the time fixed for payment by an employer, or who, for any other reason, is not paid at that time, shall be paid thereafter at any time or upon fourteen (14) days' demand. No employer shall, by any means, secure exemption from this section. History: Created 1974 Ky. Acts ch. 275, sec. 1. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Kentucky may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment schedule, delaying each payment by 90 days. This directly contradicts the intent of KRS § 337.055, as explained by the LLM, which mandates regular and timely wage payments. While the scraped snippets focus on payment upon termination, the LLM explanation broadens this to the intent of ensuring predictable compensation, especially considering the mention of union fees in the contract. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition is met because the new deadline violates state law on timely payments.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that appropriate action is not taken by the Indemnitor within thirty (30) days after the Indemnitor's receipt of written notice from the Indemnitee, the Indemnitee shall have the right to defend such Claim with counsel reasonably acceptable to the Indemnitor, and no settlement of any such Claim may be made without the prior written approval of the Indemnitor, which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that appropriate action is not taken by the Indemnitor within five (5) days after the Indemnitor's receipt of written notice from the Indemnitee, the Indemnitee shall have the right to defend such Claim with counsel reasonably acceptable to the Indemnitor, and no settlement of any such Claim may be made without the prior written approval of the Indemnitor, which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the indemnification response time to 5 days may be insufficient to conduct a reasonable investigation and respond to a claim appropriately. This modification introduces a legal contradiction because it may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in Kentucky contracts, especially insurance contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kentucky law regarding Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Kentucky law regarding Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Although no specific statute dictates the indemnification response time, Kentucky law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Setting an unreasonably short response time of 5 days for an indemnification claim may be considered a violation of this covenant, as it hinders the Indemnitor's ability to adequately investigate and respond, particularly in complex cases. While not a direct statutory violation, it increases the risk of a bad faith claim.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the response time for indemnification from 30 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Kentucky law because 5 days may not be enough time to adequately investigate a claim. While no law snippet is present to definitively prove a legal deadline exists, the reasoning provided implies that the reduced time frame creates an unreasonable burden and could lead to a bad faith claim, fitting the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties agree not to disparage or make derogatory comments, verbal or written, regarding the other Party during the Term of the Agreement, and for one year thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties agree not to disparage or make derogatory comments, verbal or written, regarding the other Party during the Term of the Agreement, and perpetually thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-disparagement clause perpetually can create legal issues, especially if it unduly restricts free speech or the ability to report unlawful activity. In Kentucky, such a broad restriction could be challenged as against public policy, particularly if it impedes the reporting of illegal or unethical behavior.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kentucky Constitution, Section 8: Freedom of speech and press
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the Kentucky Constitution guarantees freedom of speech. A perpetual non-disparagement clause could be argued to violate this constitutional right, especially if it prevents a party from making truthful statements about the other party, even if those statements are critical. While the right to free speech is not absolute, a perpetual restriction on speech must be narrowly tailored and serve a compelling interest, which a general non-disparagement clause is unlikely to satisfy, especially since the contract is based in Kentucky.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-disparagement clause perpetually, which the Law Explanation suggests could violate Section 8 of the Kentucky Constitution, guaranteeing freedom of speech. This is because a perpetual restriction on speech must be narrowly tailored and serve a compelling interest, which a general non-disparagement clause is unlikely to satisfy. The absence of scraped law snippets doesn't negate the potential conflict with free speech rights as argued in the Law Explanation.
